quality.rationale: 基于大爆炸核合成标准模型的多步因果推理，涵盖膨胀速率、弱作用冻结及氘瓶颈等核心物理机制

origin.question: 19.29 Under which of the following suppositions would the hot big bang have produced less $\mathbf{He}^4$ than predicted by the "standard" model? Less $\mathbf{H}^2$ (deuterium)?\n(v) Suppose that the gravitational constant $G$ varies with cosmological time and was slightly larger in the past.
origin.answer: A larger past $G$ increases $\\mathbf{He}^4$ production and decreases $\\mathbf{H}^2$ (deuterium) abundance.
origin.solution: In the Friedmann equation $H^2 \\propto G\rho$, a larger gravitational constant $G$ in the early universe increases the Hubble expansion rate $H$. Neutron-proton weak interactions freeze out when the reaction rate $\Gamma_{\\text{weak}} \\sim H(T)$. A higher $H$ pushes the freeze-out to a higher temperature $T_f$. Since thermal equilibrium favors neutrons at higher temperatures, a higher $T_f$ yields a larger initial neutron-to-proton ratio $n/p$. During primordial nucleosynthesis, more free neutrons are available to form helium-4, thereby increasing its mass fraction $Y_{\\text{He}}$. Additionally, the accelerated expansion and higher neutron abundance cause deuterium ($\\mathbf{H}^2$) to be processed into helium-4 more efficiently before it can survive the deuterium bottleneck. Thus, a historically larger $G$ predicts more $\\mathbf{He}^4$ and less deuterium than the standard constant-$G$ model.
